name: Monegros Canal Route
description: Cycle the 78.0-mile Route of the Monegros Canal, a moderate road cycling route through a unique, desert-like landscape.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Alcalá De Gurrea traverse the diverse landscapes of the Hoya de Huesca region in Aragon, Spain. The area is characterized by the flat expanse of the Llanos de la Violada and the presence of the Sotón river. Cyclists encounter a blend of flat and undulating terrain, with routes often featuring the scenic La Sotonera reservoir and distant views of the Sierra de Guara. Best road cycling routes around Alcalá De Gurrea The most popular road cycling route is Monegros Canal Route, a 78.0 miles (125.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 53 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows the Monegros Canal, offering a consistent ride through the plains. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Nuevo Village Entrance – Parque Miguel Servet loop from Almudévar, a difficult 58.6 miles (94.3 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain, providing a challenging experience with varied scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Parish Church of Marracos – Sotonera Reservoir loop from Marracos, a 38.6 miles (62.2 km) trail leading through the area surrounding the La Sotonera reservoir, often completed in about 3 hours 0 minutes. Road cycling around Alcalá De Gurrea is defined by the plains of La Violada, the Sotón river, and the expansive La Sotonera reservoir. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging routes with greater elevation. The routes in Alcalá De Gurrea are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alcalá De Gurrea's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Alcalá De Gurrea?
A: There are 6 road cycling routes available around Alcalá De Gurrea, offering a range of experiences from easy to difficult. These routes have been explored by over 200 road cyclists using komoot.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Alcalá De Gurrea?
A: Road cycling routes in Alcalá De Gurrea traverse the diverse landscapes of the Hoya de Huesca region. You can expect a blend of flat terrain, particularly across the Llanos de la Violada, and undulating sections. Routes often feature the scenic La Sotonera reservoir, with some segments potentially including well-maintained gravel sections alongside mostly paved surfaces.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in the area?
A: Yes, there is an easy road cycling route available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435752" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ardisa Dam loop from Club Náutico La Sotonera&lt;/a&gt; is a 34.3 km ride with moderate elevation gain, making it suitable for those looking for a less strenuous experience.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31487624" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nuevo Village Entrance – Parque Miguel Servet loop from Almudévar&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 94.3 km path with significant elevation gain, offering a demanding ride through varied scenery.

Q: Are there road cycling routes that offer scenic views or pass by notable landmarks?
A: Many routes offer scenic views, particularly those around the La Sotonera reservoir. From elevated points, such as the hermitage of Nuestra Señora de los Agudos, you can enjoy expansive views of the reservoir, the Sierra de Guara, and the distinctive Mallos de Riglos. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435355" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parish Church of Marracos – Sotonera Reservoir loop from Marracos&lt;/a&gt; is a great option for exploring the reservoir area.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Alcalá De Gurrea?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435355" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parish Church of Marracos – Sotonera Reservoir loop from Marracos&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435960" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valpalmas – Sotonera Reservoir loop from Gurrea de Gállego&lt;/a&gt; are excellent circular options that allow you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Alcalá De Gurrea?
A: The Hoya de Huesca region generally offers a pleasant climate for cycling. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, with milder temperatures and less extreme weather. Summer can be hot, especially in the plains, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during those months.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Alcalá De Gurrea?
A: The road cycling routes around Alcalá De Gurrea are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from the flat plains to the scenic reservoir views, and the opportunities for both moderate and challenging rides.

Q: Are there any specific attractions or points of interest I can visit along the routes?
A: Absolutely. The region boasts several points of interest. You can cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3562656" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sotonera Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5857443" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ardisa Dam&lt;/a&gt;, or explore the unique geological formations of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/558941" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguarales de Valdemilaz&lt;/a&gt;. The historic settlement of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6362271" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almudévar&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5284575" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Atalaya (watchtower in La Sotonera)&lt;/a&gt; are also nearby.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, for those looking for longer rides, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926261201" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monegros Canal Route&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 125.6 km trail. This moderate route follows the Monegros Canal, offering a consistent ride through the plains and is typically completed in under 5 hours.

Q: Are there routes that focus on the La Sotonera reservoir?
A: The La Sotonera reservoir is a central feature for many routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435355" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parish Church of Marracos – Sotonera Reservoir loop from Marracos&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435960" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valpalmas – Sotonera Reservoir loop from Gurrea de Gállego&lt;/a&gt; both offer excellent opportunities to cycle around and enjoy views of this significant body of water.

Q: What is the average duration for road cycling routes in this area?
A: The duration of road cycling routes varies significantly based on length and difficulty. For example, an easy route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31435752" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ardisa Dam loop&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in just over an hour and a half, while longer, more challenging routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e926261201" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monegros Canal Route&lt;/a&gt; can take nearly five hours.
tips: This reservoir on the Sotón River was created by damming the river to serve as the source of the Canal de los Monegros.

In the middle of the desert, the Canal de Los Monegros serves to irrigate an area of 110,000 hectares. It takes water from the Sotonera reservoir and transfers it to irrigate Los Monegros, La Sobrarbe and Somontano, in the provinces of Huesca and Zaragoza.

The Charterhouse of Our Lady of the Fountains, also known as the Charterhouse of Monegros, is a former monastery located in the municipality of Sariñena, in the region of Monegros, province of Huesca, Aragon. Founded in 1507 by the Counts of Sástago, Beatriz de Luna, and Blasco de Aragón, it is the first Charterhouse built in the Kingdom of Aragon.

The current monastery, built in the 18th century, is an impressive architectural complex in the late Baroque style. It is notable for its mural paintings by Friar Manuel Bayeu, Goya's brother-in-law, depicting scenes from the life of the Virgin Mary, women of the Old Testament, and the cardinal virtues.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Route of the Monegros Canal?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily asphalt, covering about 125 km of paved surfaces. However, it also includes around 409 meters of unpaved sections, with an additional 139 meters of unknown surface type. While a road bike can handle much of it, some parts might be more suited for gravel bikes or require careful handling.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Route of the Monegros Canal for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: It's generally considered a "moderate road ride" requiring good fitness. The unpaved sections contribute to this difficulty, as they can be challenging for a typical road bike and may require you to dismount and push your bicycle in certain areas.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and landmarks can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the arid, desert-like landscape of the Monegros region, characterized by ochre colors and a solitary atmosphere. Look out for unique geological formations like the "torrollones" (multi-colored rock formations). The route also passes by the La Sotonera Reservoir, the Canal de los Monegros itself, and the Charterhouse of Our Lady of Las Fuentes.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Monegros Canal route?
faqs.answer: The Monegros region can be very hot in summer and quite cold in winter. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the extremes and strong winds that can occur.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle the Route of the Monegros Canal?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 125.6 km long and typically takes around 4 hours and 53 minutes to complete on a race bike, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Route of the Monegros Canal?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information for the trailhead is not provided, but generally, you would look for parking in nearby towns or designated areas close to where you plan to start your ride along the canal.

faqs.question: Is the Route of the Monegros Canal dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific restrictions mentioned for dogs on this route, it's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in agricultural areas or near wildlife. Be mindful of the arid conditions and ensure your dog has plenty of water.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to cycle the Route of the Monegros Canal. It is generally accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Is a road bike suitable for the entire Monegros Canal route?
faqs.answer: While the route is designated for race bikes, be aware that there are unpaved sections. A road bike can manage most of the asphalt, but you might find some unpaved parts challenging and may need to dismount or proceed with caution. A gravel bike might offer a more comfortable experience on the mixed terrain.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other official trails, including the Camino Natural de Pedro Saputo, Camino de la Jorgeada, Camino de Alcalá de Gurrea, and Camino del Puente, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, water, or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: The Monegros region can be quite solitary. It's recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, as amenities like cafes or shops might be sparse directly along the canal. Plan your stops in advance in the towns you pass through or near the start/end points for food and accommodation.

waypoints.name: Charterhouse of Our Lady of Las Fuentes
